questions.text: ENT A young male presents with unilateral epistaxis. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Unilateral epistaxis in a young male (adolescent) is a classic presentation of juvenile nasal angiofibroma (JNA), a vascular tumor requiring rule-out by nasal endoscopy. Septal deviation and foreign body typically cause obstruction rather than recurrent bleeding, and allergic rhinitis/nasal polyps are usually bilateral.

questions.text: A patient presents with hearing loss. What type of hearing loss is indicated if it's conductive in nature on the right side?
questions.explanation: Conductive hearing loss localizes the problem to the external or middle ear on the affected side; since the pathology is specified as right-sided and conductive, the diagnosis is right conductive hearing loss. Sensorineural options are excluded since the defect is described as conductive, not inner ear/nerve related.

questions.text: What tuning fork frequency is used for hearing tests?
questions.explanation: The standard tuning fork used for Weber's and Rinne's tests is 512 Hz, providing a balance between adequate duration of vibration and clinical accuracy. Lower frequencies (128–256 Hz) are felt more than heard, while higher frequencies decay too quickly for reliable bedside testing.

questions.text: A child presents with a midline neck lump that moves when they stick out their tongue. What is the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: A thyroglossal cyst is a midline neck lump that characteristically moves upward on tongue protrusion due to its embryological connection to the foramen cecum. Thyroid isthmus swelling moves with swallowing but not tongue protrusion, dermoid cysts are non-tender and mobile without this movement, and branchial cysts are lateral, not midline.

questions.text: Forensic Which of the following is NOT an early sign of death?
questions.explanation: Livor mortis, rigor mortis, and pallor mortis are recognized postmortem changes that develop progressively over time and are used forensically to estimate time and circumstances of death. Loss of peripheral pulse occurs immediately at the moment of cardiac arrest rather than developing afterward, so it is a clinical sign of death, not a postmortem change. This distinction is why the lecture emphasized rigor mortis, hypostasis, and decomposition as the key diagnostic postmortem changes.

questions.text: Traumatic asphyxia results from severe injury to which part of the body?
questions.explanation: Traumatic asphyxia is defined as compression on the chest and abdomen by external force, impairing the mechanical ability to breathe. This differs from strangulation/hanging (neck level) and smothering (mouth and nose level), which are other forms of violent asphyxia acting at different anatomical levels.

questions.text: In forensic medicine, strangulation can be categorized into which manner of death?
questions.explanation: Strangulation (compression of neck structures by ligature or hands) is mostly homicidal, unlike hanging, which is mostly suicidal but can be accidental or homicidal. This distinction in manner of death is forensically important because it directly affects legal classification and investigation of the case.

questions.text: Which type of injury is not caused by a blunt object but rather by a sharp object, typically resulting in clean, well-defined wound edges?
questions.explanation: Incisions/cut wounds are caused by a clean, sharp-edged object such as a knife or glass splinter, producing regular edges, clear margins, and no tissue bridging. In contrast, lacerations, abrasions, and contusions all result from blunt trauma and typically show irregular edges, contused margins, and tissue bridging.

questions.text: What is the approximate time window during which myocardial cells can be harvested after cardiac arrest?
questions.explanation: Different tissues and organs remain viable for different durations after death, since cells do not die instantly and organs vary in their tolerance to anoxia (the lecture notes the brain survives only 5-7 minutes and the kidney about 45 minutes). This variability explains why timing is critical for organ/tissue donation and harvesting after cardiac arrest.

questions.text: Stab wounds can be classified into which of the following categories?
questions.explanation: Stab wounds can result from a purely sharp instrument, a blunt instrument, or a blunt object with a pointed tip (as illustrated by the example of a worker falling onto a round-tipped iron rod), since what matters legally and clinically is the depth and mechanism of penetration rather than the object's overall sharpness. This is why all three categories are considered valid classifications of stab wounds in forensic assessment.

questions.text: ENT Which ossicle's footplate is attached directly to the oval window, transmitting sound into the inner ear?
questions.explanation: The stapes footplate sits on the oval window and pushes on it to generate perilymph waves in the cochlea, continuing the sound conduction pathway. The malleus and incus are earlier links in the ossicular chain but do not directly contact the oval window.

questions.text: A 70-year-old poorly controlled diabetic presents with severe otalgia and granulation tissue in the ear canal. Which diagnosis must be suspected?
questions.explanation: Severe otalgia in an elderly patient with uncontrolled diabetes is a classic red flag for necrotizing (malignant) otitis externa, an aggressive infection that can extend to the skull base. Acute otitis externa and media are far more common but do not carry this specific risk profile of severe pain plus uncontrolled diabetes.

questions.text: Shedding of otoconia (crystals) from the utricle into the semicircular canals is the underlying mechanism of which condition?
questions.explanation: BPPV results from displaced otoconia (crystals) from the utricle entering the semicircular canals, causing brief seconds-long vertigo with position change. Ménière's disease involves endolymphatic hydrops instead, and vestibular neuritis is due to inflammation, not crystal displacement.

questions.text: A patient reports recurrent vertigo lasting hours, associated with fluctuating unilateral hearing loss, tinnitus, and aural fullness. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The triad of episodic vertigo (hours), fluctuating sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus, and aural fullness is characteristic of Ménière's disease (endolymphatic hydrops). BPPV lasts seconds and vestibular neuritis lasts days, and neither is typically associated with hearing loss.

questions.text: Vesicles in the ear canal accompanied by ipsilateral facial nerve palsy should raise suspicion for which condition?
questions.explanation: Ramsay Hunt syndrome (herpes zoster oticus) presents with vesicles in the ear canal, facial nerve palsy, and sometimes vertigo or sensorineural hearing loss. This combination of vesicles plus facial palsy is not typical of the other otological conditions listed.

questions.text: On Weber's test, sound lateralizes to the right ear. Rinne's test on the right shows bone conduction greater than air conduction. What is the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Lateralization to the affected ear on Weber's test combined with a negative Rinne's test (bone conduction greater than air conduction) on that same side confirms a conductive hearing loss in the right ear. Sensorineural loss would instead lateralize away from the affected ear with a positive Rinne's test.

questions.text: What is the primary function of the Eustachian tube?
options: Transmission of vibration to the cochlea
Ventilation and equalization of middle ear pressure
originalOptions: Transmission of vibration to the cochlea
Ventilation and equalization of middle ear pressure
questions.correctAnswer: D) Ventilation and equalization of middle ear pressure
questions.correctAnswerText: Ventilation and equalization of middle ear pressure
questions.explanation: The Eustachian tube connects the middle ear to the nasopharynx and functions to ventilate the middle ear space, equalizing its pressure with atmospheric pressure. Sound amplification and vibration transmission are functions of the ossicles, not the Eustachian tube.

questions.text: During an emergency airway obstruction, a cricothyrotomy is performed through which structure?
questions.explanation: The cricothyroid membrane, located between the thyroid and cricoid cartilages, is the site used for emergency cricothyrotomy due to its superficial and relatively avascular location. The cricoid cartilage and thyroid isthmus are nearby structures but are not the incision site for this procedure.

questions.text: A 55-year-old smoker presents with hoarseness of voice persisting for more than 3 weeks. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Start proton pump inhibitor and observe
Voice rest and follow-up in 1 month
Urgent referral to ENT for fiberoptic laryngoscopy
originalOptions: Start proton pump inhibitor and observe
Voice rest and follow-up in 1 month
Urgent referral to ENT for fiberoptic laryngoscopy
questions.correctAnswer: E) Urgent referral to ENT for fiberoptic laryngoscopy
questions.correctAnswerText: Urgent referral to ENT for fiberoptic laryngoscopy
questions.explanation: Persistent hoarseness beyond 3 weeks in a smoker is a red flag for laryngeal malignancy and warrants urgent ENT referral for fiberoptic endoscopy to visualize the vocal cords and rule out tumors. Reassurance, antibiotics, or symptomatic measures risk delaying diagnosis of a potentially neoplastic process.

questions.text: Tenderness elicited on palpation of the tragus is most suggestive of which condition?
questions.explanation: Tragal tenderness on palpation is a classic clinical sign of otitis externa, as inflammation of the external auditory canal makes movement of the pinna/tragus painful. Acute otitis media, a middle ear condition, does not typically produce tragal tenderness since the external ear is not primarily inflamed.
